prompt: Name a song you find genuinely moving — any language, any era — and describe, in a short paragraph, why it works on you.
response: "**Hallelujah**" by Leonard Cohen, particularly Jeff Buckley's rendition, never fails to move me. Its power lies in the collision between its sacred title and its deeply human, flawed, and sensual narrative. The song weaves together biblical imagery (David, Samson, Bathsheba) with raw, personal confessions of love, doubt, and brokenness. The word "Hallelujah" itself becomes ambiguous—not just a cry of praise, but one of resignation, awe, and even despair. Buckley's aching, vulnerable vocal delivery turns the song into a solitary prayer in an empty room. It works because it acknowledges that transcendence and wreckage often come from the same source, and that a broken "Hallelujah" can be the most honest kind.
